edition: Verbal Reasoning Full Mock · Original v1
rightsNotice: 本卷依据当前 UCAT Verbal Reasoning 的公开结构独立命题，不复制、改写或影射官方 Question Bank 或 Practice Test；只报告 44 分原始正确数，不换算 300–900 分。

sourceAnchors.usedFor: current four-subtest identity and cycle boundary only
official preparation sequence and interface terminology only

passages.title: Restoring a tidal marsh
paragraphs: A coastal council removed an old embankment from part of a former grazing field so that seawater could return at high tide. During the first three years, salt-tolerant plants spread across most of the site and winter counts recorded more wading birds than before the work. The council also installed a new inland bank to protect nearby homes.

The monitoring team measured vegetation and birds but did not measure changes in fish numbers. Two footpaths were closed because they flooded regularly; a raised path opened along the inland bank. Farmers upstream reported no change in the frequency of flooding on their land. The project report says that longer monitoring is needed before judging how much carbon the new marsh stores.

passages.title: A weekend night-bus trial
paragraphs: A university funded buses between its campus and three residential districts on Friday and Saturday nights for one term. Passenger numbers were highest in the first four weeks and then stabilised. Most journeys began between 11 pm and 1 am. In an online survey sent to all students, 46 per cent of respondents said they had used the service at least once.

The survey did not ask non-users why they had avoided the buses. Security staff recorded fewer requests for escorted walks on trial nights than on the same nights in the previous term, although the report notes that weather and student numbers differed between the two periods. The university has not decided whether fares would be charged if the service continues.

passages.title: Testing stored seeds
paragraphs: A regional seed bank stores duplicate samples of rare plants in two buildings. Each year, technicians test a small number of seeds from every sample. If too few germinate, they grow new plants and collect fresh seed. In 2025, samples kept at lower humidity generally retained higher germination rates than samples stored under the older conditions.

The pattern was not identical for every species. Some seeds naturally survive drying poorly, and three wetland plants performed better under the older method. The bank will not move all samples to the lower-humidity rooms. Instead, it plans to use species-specific storage rules and to repeat tests after equipment in one building is replaced.

passages.title: Bilingual station signs
paragraphs: A railway added bilingual signs at six stations in a region where two languages are widely used. Researchers observed travellers before and after installation. Requests for directions fell at four stations, remained similar at one, and increased slightly at the busiest interchange. The researchers could not identify the language used in every request.

Interviews suggested that many local passengers valued seeing both languages, while some visitors found longer place names harder to scan quickly. The report recommends testing type size, contrast and placement rather than assuming that adding more text automatically improves navigation. No station removed its existing audio announcements during the study.

passages.title: A shared solar scheme
paragraphs: Residents who could not install panels on their own roofs bought shares in a solar array on a warehouse. Electricity from the array was sold to the grid, and members received a credit linked to their share. In the first year, generation was 8 per cent below the forecast because two winter months were unusually cloudy.

Administrative costs were higher than expected, but the scheme still issued a small credit to every member. The organiser negotiated a cheaper maintenance contract for year two. Membership was open to tenants and homeowners within the district, although the minimum share price remained beyond the budget of some households. The scheme did not supply electricity directly to members' homes.

passages.title: Light and sleep timing
paragraphs: Researchers asked forty adults to spend four evenings in a laboratory. On two evenings participants read from a bright tablet; on two they read the same text from printed pages under dim light. Tablet evenings were followed by a later average release of a hormone associated with sleep timing, and participants reported feeling less sleepy at the scheduled bedtime.

The order of conditions was varied between participants. The study did not test children, shift workers or people using their own devices at home. Participants went to bed at the same laboratory time after every session, so the researchers did not observe whether they would voluntarily have stayed awake longer. The report calls the findings evidence of a short-term effect under controlled conditions.

passages.title: Dating pottery at a hill settlement
paragraphs: Archaeologists found fragments of painted pottery in three layers of a hill settlement. Similar decoration is usually associated with the late eighth century, but charcoal from the lowest layer produced an earlier radiocarbon range. The team says the pottery may have entered an older layer through a later pit, although the edge of such a pit was not visible during excavation.

A second possibility is that the decorative style began earlier in this valley than at sites used to establish the usual date. The report does not choose conclusively between the explanations. It recommends analysis of clay sources and further dating of short-lived plant remains from sealed contexts.

passages.title: Measuring restaurant food waste
paragraphs: Twelve restaurants weighed edible food discarded from plates for six weeks. During the next six weeks, half offered smaller default portions with free additional servings, while the others kept their menus unchanged. Average plate waste fell at five of the six restaurants using smaller defaults and at two comparison restaurants.

Customers requested additional servings in fewer than one in ten meals at the smaller-default restaurants. The study did not record whether customers later bought food elsewhere. Participating restaurants volunteered and all were located in one city. Researchers concluded that smaller defaults were promising but that costs, customer satisfaction and results in other settings should be examined.

passages.title: Tracking urban foxes
paragraphs: Biologists fitted GPS collars to eighteen foxes from two neighbouring districts. Foxes in the district with more connected gardens travelled shorter nightly distances on average than foxes separated by major roads. The collars recorded location every fifteen minutes for five months, but four collars stopped transmitting early.

The researchers mapped bins and feeding sites but could not determine how much food each fox obtained from them. They warn that the two districts differed in several ways besides garden connectivity. The findings show an association, not proof that opening routes between gardens would reduce every fox's travel.

passages.title: Remote interpreting in court
paragraphs: A court service compared hearings using interpreters in the courtroom with hearings using interpreters by secure video. Remote connections reduced travel cancellations, but technical interruptions occurred in 7 per cent of remote hearings. Judges postponed two hearings because communication could not be restored.

Participants were asked whether they could understand the interpretation. Reported understanding was similar in the two groups, although response rates were lower among defendants than among lawyers. The review did not examine case outcomes or whether participants felt equally able to speak privately with their representatives. It recommends retaining remote interpreting for suitable hearings while providing a rapid switch to an in-person or alternative connection when communication fails.

passages.title: School gardens and science lessons
paragraphs: Eight primary schools created gardens for use in science lessons. Teachers received the same set of lesson plans, but each school decided how often to use them. After one year, pupils at six schools showed higher scores on a plant-science quiz than the previous year's pupils. Two schools used the garden fewer than three times because of staff changes.

The project did not include schools without gardens, and the two pupil cohorts were not identical. Teachers reported that practical activities helped explain pollination and soil, but several found maintenance difficult during holidays. The evaluators recommend a controlled comparison and dedicated maintenance support before claiming that gardens alone improve science attainment.

questions.prompt: Wading-bird counts were higher after the embankment was removed.
The project measured an increase in fish numbers.
The restored marsh will store more carbon in year ten than in year five.
Which conclusion is best supported?
options: Every former field should be restored as marsh.
The project caused more flooding upstream.
The project produced early ecological changes but some effects still require longer monitoring.
All previous footpath access was preserved.

questions.prompt: Most recorded journeys began after 1 am.
Fewer escorted walks were requested on trial nights than on the comparison nights.
Charging a fare would reduce passenger numbers by at least 20 per cent.
Which statement best summarises the evidence?
options: The trial proves the buses caused the fall in escorted walks.
The service had users, but the comparison cannot isolate every cause and future fares are undecided.
All students used the service at least once.
Passenger numbers continued falling throughout the term.

questions.prompt: Every species retained more viable seed at lower humidity.
The bank stores duplicate samples in two buildings.
Equipment replacement will improve germination for all wetland species.
Which action is supported by the passage?
options: Adopt one storage rule for every species.
Discard samples that perform better under the older method.
Use species-specific rules and repeat testing after equipment changes.

questions.prompt: Requests for directions fell at all six stations.
Audio announcements remained in place during the study.
The increase at the interchange was caused by the bilingual signs.
Which conclusion is best supported?
options: Longer names should never appear on station signs.
Sign design should be tested because adding text has mixed navigation effects.
Audio announcements made the written signs unnecessary.

questions.prompt: Generation in year one exceeded the forecast by 8 per cent.
Every member received a credit in the first year.
The cheaper maintenance contract will make the year-two credit larger.
Which statement is best supported?
options: The scheme delivered electricity directly to each home.
The scheme widened eligibility but the minimum price still excluded some households.
Cloudy weather prevented any member credit.

questions.prompt: Participants read the same text in the tablet and print conditions.
Tablet evenings were followed by earlier average hormone release.
Children would show a larger effect than adults.
Which limitation is stated?
options: The order of conditions was identical for every participant.
Participants chose their own bedtime after each session.
The reading material differed between conditions.
The study did not show whether participants would voluntarily remain awake longer.

questions.prompt: The lowest layer produced an earlier radiocarbon range than the usual date for the decoration.
Excavators could clearly see the edge of a later pit.
Clay analysis will prove that the pottery entered through a later pit.
Which statement best reflects the report?
options: The conflict has two possible explanations and further evidence is needed.
Radiocarbon dating is always less reliable than decorative style.
The pottery definitely belongs to the lowest layer.
Decorative styles began at the same time in every valley.

questions.prompt: Plate waste fell at every restaurant using smaller default portions.
Additional servings were requested in fewer than 10 per cent of meals at smaller-default restaurants.
Customers at smaller-default restaurants ate fewer calories over the whole day.
Which conclusion is best supported?
options: The intervention is promising, but voluntary one-city participation limits generalisation.
Comparison restaurants did not reduce waste.
Customer satisfaction was higher in every intervention restaurant.

questions.prompt: Four collars stopped transmitting before the study ended.
Foxes in the more connected district travelled farther on average.
Every fox obtained most of its food from bins.
Which conclusion is best supported?
options: Connected gardens have been proved to reduce fox travel.
Roads were the only difference between the districts.
The data show an association but do not establish a single cause.
Opening every garden route would reduce every fox's journey.

questions.prompt: Technical interruptions occurred in 7 per cent of remote hearings.
Reported understanding was lower in the remote group.
Remote interpreting produced the same case outcomes as in-person interpreting.
Which recommendation matches the review?
options: Use remote interpreting for every hearing without backup.
Retain remote use where suitable and provide a rapid alternative when communication fails.
End all remote interpreting because two hearings were postponed.

questions.prompt: All eight schools used the gardens regularly throughout the year.
Six schools recorded higher plant-science quiz scores than the previous year's pupils.
Dedicated holiday maintenance would guarantee higher science attainment.
Which conclusion is best supported?
options: The project proves that gardens alone improve attainment.
Practical activities were unpopular with teachers.
The results justify removing classroom science lessons.
The observations are encouraging but need a controlled comparison and maintenance support.
